title: Queue

sections.title: Introduction to Queue
concepts: Queue follows FIFO (First In First Out) principle
Elements are added at the rear and removed from the front
Real-world analogies: queue at ticket counter, printer queue, CPU task scheduling
Queue has two ends: front (for deletion) and rear (for insertion)
Applications of queues in computing: print spooling, CPU scheduling, BFS in graphs
Difference between stack (LIFO) and queue (FIFO)

definitions.definition: A queue is a linear data structure that follows the FIFO (First In First Out) principle. Elements are inserted at the rear end and removed from the front end.
FIFO stands for First In First Out. It means the element that is inserted first is the first one to be removed. The oldest element in the queue is always at the front.
Front is the end of the queue from which elements are removed (dequeued). It points to the first (oldest) element in the queue.
Rear is the end of the queue at which new elements are added (enqueued). It points to the last (newest) element in the queue.

key_points: Queue follows FIFO — the first element inserted is the first to be removed
Insertion happens at the rear end; deletion happens at the front end
A queue has two pointers: front and rear
Queue is different from a stack (LIFO) — stack uses one end, queue uses two ends
Applications: job scheduling, print queue, BFS traversal, handling requests in web servers

sections.title: Operations on Queue
concepts: Enqueue — adds an element at the rear of the queue
Dequeue — removes and returns the element from the front of the queue
Peek/Front — returns the front element without removing it
isEmpty — checks if the queue is empty
size — returns the number of elements in the queue
Queue overflow — inserting into a full queue
Queue underflow — removing from an empty queue

definitions.definition: Enqueue is the operation of adding (inserting) a new element at the rear end of the queue. After enqueue, the new element becomes the last element and the rear pointer is updated.
Dequeue is the operation of removing (deleting) an element from the front end of the queue. The element at the front is removed and returned, and the front pointer is updated.
Peek (or front) is the operation of viewing the front element of the queue without removing it. It returns the element that would be dequeued next.

formulas.name: Enqueue algorithm
formulas.formula:
Step 1: If queue is full, print 'Overflow' and return
Step 2: Increment rear by 1
Step 3: Set queue[rear] = element
Step 4: If front is -1, set front = 0
Step 5: Return
formulas.notes: In Python using list, we use append() which adds to the end automatically

formulas.name: Dequeue algorithm
formulas.formula:
Step 1: If queue is empty, print 'Underflow' and return
Step 2: Set element = queue[front]
Step 3: Increment front by 1
Step 4: If front > rear, reset front = rear = -1 (queue empty)
Step 5: Return element
formulas.notes: In Python using list, we use pop(0) which removes the first element

key_points: Enqueue adds an element at the rear; dequeue removes an element from the front
Before enqueue, check for overflow (queue full)
Before dequeue, check for underflow (queue empty)
Peek returns the front element without removing it
In Python, append() is used for enqueue and pop(0) is used for dequeue

sections.title: Implementation of Queue Using Python
concepts: Using Python list to implement a queue
append() method for enqueue operation
pop(0) method for dequeue operation
Accessing front element using index 0
Checking empty queue using len() == 0
Implementing queue operations as functions
Menu-driven program for queue operations
Limitation of pop(0) — O(n) time complexity

formulas.name: Queue implementation using list
formulas.formula:
queue = []  # empty queue
queue.append(element)  # Enqueue
element = queue.pop(0)  # Dequeue
front = queue[0]  # Peek
len(queue)  # Size
len(queue) == 0  # isEmpty
formulas.notes: pop(0) is O(n) — for better performance, use collections.deque

key_points: Python list can be used to implement a queue
append() adds to the rear of the queue — enqueue operation
pop(0) removes from the front of the queue — dequeue operation
queue[0] gives the front element — peek operation
len(queue) == 0 checks if the queue is empty
Always check for underflow (empty queue) before dequeue
pop(0) shifts all elements, making it O(n) — collections.deque is more efficient

sections.title: Introduction to Deque
concepts: Deque stands for Double-Ended Queue
Elements can be added or removed from both front and rear
Deque is a generalization of both stack and queue
Input-restricted deque — insertion only at one end, deletion from both ends
Output-restricted deque — deletion only at one end, insertion at both ends
Deque supports all operations of both stack and queue
Applications: palindrome checking, sliding window problems

definitions.definition: A deque (pronounced 'deck') is a linear data structure that allows insertion and deletion of elements from both the front and the rear end. It is a generalization of both stack and queue.
An input-restricted deque is a deque where insertion is allowed only at one end (rear), but deletion is allowed from both ends (front and rear).
An output-restricted deque is a deque where deletion is allowed only at one end (front), but insertion is allowed at both ends (front and rear).

key_points: Deque allows insertion and deletion from both front and rear ends
A deque can function as both a stack and a queue
Input-restricted deque: insert at one end, delete from both ends
Output-restricted deque: delete from one end, insert at both ends
Deque is more flexible than both stack and queue

sections.title: Implementation of Deque Using Python
concepts: Using Python list to implement a deque
insert(0, element) for adding at front
append(element) for adding at rear
pop(0) for removing from front
pop() for removing from rear
Implementing deque operations as functions
Using collections.deque for efficient implementation
Menu-driven program for deque operations

formulas.name: Deque implementation using list
formulas.formula:
deque = []  # empty deque
deque.append(element)  # Add at rear
deque.insert(0, element)  # Add at front
element = deque.pop()  # Remove from rear
element = deque.pop(0)  # Remove from front
formulas.notes: insert(0, elem) and pop(0) are O(n). For O(1), use collections.deque with appendleft() and popleft()

key_points: Python list can implement a deque using append(), insert(0,), pop(), and pop(0)
append() adds at the rear, insert(0, element) adds at the front
pop() removes from the rear, pop(0) removes from the front
List-based deque has O(n) operations for front operations
collections.deque provides O(1) operations: appendleft(), popleft(), append(), pop()
A deque can simulate both stack (use one end) and queue (use both ends)

summary: A queue is a linear data structure that follows the FIFO (First In First Out) principle.
Elements are added at the rear end (enqueue) and removed from the front end (dequeue).
Queue overflow occurs when inserting into a full queue; underflow occurs when removing from an empty queue.
In Python, a queue can be implemented using a list: append() for enqueue and pop(0) for dequeue.
A deque (double-ended queue) allows insertion and deletion from both the front and rear ends.
A deque is a generalization of both stack and queue.
An input-restricted deque allows insertion at only one end; an output-restricted deque allows deletion at only one end.
In Python, a deque can be implemented using a list with append(), insert(0,), pop(), and pop(0).
For efficient deque operations, the collections.deque class provides O(1) operations for both ends.

questions.question: Differentiate between a stack and a queue.
questions.answer:
Stack:
- Follows LIFO (Last In First Out) principle
- Insertion (PUSH) and deletion (POP) happen at the same end (TOP)
- Uses a single pointer (TOP)
- Example: stack of plates, undo operation

Queue:
- Follows FIFO (First In First Out) principle
- Insertion (enqueue) happens at the rear; deletion (dequeue) happens at the front
- Uses two pointers (front and rear)
- Example: queue at a ticket counter, print queue

questions.question: What is a deque? How is it different from a simple queue?
questions.answer:
A deque (double-ended queue) is a linear data structure that allows insertion and deletion from both the front and rear ends.

Differences from a simple queue:
- Simple queue: insertion at rear only, deletion at front only
- Deque: insertion and deletion at both front and rear
- Simple queue supports only enqueue (rear) and dequeue (front)
- Deque supports add_front, add_rear, remove_front, remove_rear
- A deque can function as both a stack and a queue
- Simple queue is more restrictive than a deque

questions.question:
Write a Python program to implement a queue using a list with the following functions:
(a) enqueue(queue, element) — to add an element
(b) dequeue(queue) — to remove and return the front element
(c) peek(queue) — to return the front element without removing
(d) display(queue) — to display all elements
(e) isEmpty(queue) — to check if the queue is empty

What will be the output of the following code?
queue = []
queue.append(1)
queue.append(2)
queue.append(3)
print(queue.pop(0))
queue.append(4)
print(queue.pop(0))
print(queue)
questions.answer:
Output:
1
2
[3, 4]

Explanation:
- append(1) -> queue = [1]
- append(2) -> queue = [1, 2]
- append(3) -> queue = [1, 2, 3]
- pop(0) returns 1 -> queue = [2, 3] -> prints 1
- append(4) -> queue = [2, 3, 4]
- pop(0) returns 2 -> queue = [3, 4] -> prints 2
- print(queue) -> prints [3, 4]

questions.question: Write a Python program to implement a deque using a list with functions to add and remove elements from both ends.
Write a Python program to implement a queue of customer orders in a restaurant. Each order has an order number and item name. Implement enqueue, dequeue, and display using a menu-driven approach.
List any four applications of queues in computer science.
questions.answer:
Four applications of queues in computer science:
1. CPU Scheduling: Operating systems use queues to manage processes waiting for CPU time (e.g., round-robin scheduling).
2. Print Spooling: Print jobs are placed in a queue and processed in FIFO order by the printer.
3. Breadth-First Search (BFS): BFS algorithm in graphs and trees uses a queue to explore nodes level by level.
4. Handling Requests in Web Servers: Incoming HTTP requests are placed in a queue and handled one by one.

Other applications: keyboard buffer, call center systems, traffic management, message queues in distributed systems.
